summary: I was born and lived in Romania with my husband and our daughter Aida. We even started a family business there before deciding to immigrate. Romania was our home and the place where we built our early family life.

My husband is Egyptian, and although we lived in Romania, his heritage is an important part of our family story. We met and married before settling in Romania, blending our backgrounds as we raised our daughter.

After winning the Diversity Immigrant Visa Lottery, we decided to move to the United States in early 2002. It was a big change, arriving with no friends or family, but we quickly settled in, found jobs, and built a new life. Over 15 years, the U.S. has become our home where we raised three children and fulfilled our American dream.

My husband first read about the Diversity Immigrant Visa Lottery in a newspaper and we applied mostly as a joke. Nearly a year later, we were shocked to receive an official letter saying we had won. This event changed our lives, leading us to apply for visas and eventually immigrate to the U.S.

We went through a long visa application process that culminated in an interview at the United States Embassy on September 4th, 2001. The visa officer was kind and even joked with our five-year-old daughter, Aida. Receiving the visas there was a moment of great relief and hope for our future.

During our visa interview at the U.S. Embassy, the officer told our daughter Aida that she had to visit Disneyland someday. That comment stuck with us as a symbol of the new opportunities and experiences awaiting us in America. Disneyland became a hopeful image of the life we were about to start.

Our visa interview was just a week before the September 11 attacks in 2001, which shocked and pained us deeply. This tragic event made us question whether immigrating to the U.S. was the right choice, but after reflection, we decided to proceed. It was a difficult time that tested our resolve to start a new life there.

We arrived in the United States in January 2002 through Detroit Metropolitan Wayne County Airport. Landing there marked the beginning of our new life, even though we had no contacts or support. From that point, we slowly built our home, found jobs, and settled into American life.
